pages.text: Geopolymer synthesis using alkaline activation of natural zeolite C. Villa a,1, E.T. Pecina a,b,*,1, R. Torres a,1, L. Gómez c,2 a CIMAV,S.C., Compl. Ind. Chihuahua, C.P. 31109, Chihuahua, Chih., Mexico b ESI-UAdeC, Blvd. Adolfo López Mateos S/N C.P. 26830, Nueva Rosita, Coah., Mexico c FIME-UANL, Ciudad universitaria, San Nicolás, Nuevo león, Mexico article info Article history: Received 7 February 2009 Received in revised form 23 March 2010 Accepted 2 April 2010 Keywords: Alkali-activated cements Zeolite Geopolymer Compressive strength abstract The inﬂuence of activator ratio, time, and curing temperature on the mechanical strength of geopolymers synthesized from natural zeolite was evaluated by employing silicate solutions and sodium hydroxide. The results demonstrated that an increase in the activator ratio as well as that of curing time favors mechanical strength in the material. Nevertheless, with 90 day of curing time and 80 /C176C or a greater cur- ing temperature, have a negative effect on strength. Through characterization by X-ray diffraction a slight shift along with an increase in the amorphous halo between 20 /C176and 30 /C176(2h) was observed and is char- acteristic of geopolymers. SEM–EDS analysis indicated the presence of Na and an increase in the Si/Al ratio from 8 to 11.5 with the incorporation of activators. The results indicate that the use of alkali and silicate activators on non commercial zeolite could be use in the synthesis of geopolymers. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Currently, concrete based on Portland cement (PC) is the most used construction material, given that it is more economical (com- pared to metals and other materials). One of the negative aspects of PC is that its fabrication generates CO 2 through calcination of raw materials and fuel consumption. It is calculated that 1 ton of PC generates 1 ton of CO 2 [1]. It is estimated that with demographic growth and industrialization, the pollution generated by cement production in a few years will represent 17% of worldwide CO 2 emissions (currently it is around 7%) [2]. Geopolymers which are based on aluminosilicate ores presents a viable alternative for replacing PC. Geopolymers have been con- sidered the cements of the future, with their development arising in the latter part of the 1970’s and being fostered by the work of Davidovits [3]. These materials are fabricated with natural raw materials and industrial wastes that have a rich silico-aluminate composition. It is estimated that producing cement by means of geopolymerization could result in an 86% decrease in CO 2 emis- sions for each ton of PC. Geopolymeric products are made up of a tridimensional Si–Al network of linked SiO 4 and AlO 4 tetrahedrons which alternately share oxygen [4]. The empirical formula is Mn{–(SiO 2)z–AlO 2}n, wH2O, where M = k +,N a +,C a ++; where, n is the degree of polycon- densation and z = 1, 2, 3 and indicates the number of siloxane bonds [5]. Due to the fact that aluminosilicates are the compounds most abundant in the earth’s crust, there exist a large number of raw material sources rich in alumina and silicon with the potential for producing geopolymers. Among the materials with which have been used for procuring geopolymers predominates ﬂying ash (FA) [5,6] and include calcined clays [7], tailings [8,9], kaolin [10], and puzzolans [11,12]. These works have shown that geopolymeriza- tion with calcined materials promote quick dissolutio

interest is shared by the mining industry due to the extensive area of aluminosilicates material deposits and the search for new mar- kets and products for their application. Ore deposits are character- ized as heterogeneous composites of deposits with different chemical characteristics and degree of crystallization. Due to this fact, some zeolite deposits are partially exploited or do not have a commercial application. Zeolites are crystalline aluminosilicates with a structure based on tetrahedral units of silicon–oxygen (SiO4) and aluminum–oxygen (AlO 4) [12]. These materials contain interchangeable alkaline cations of earths such as: Na +,K +,C a2+ and Mg2+ which keep a natural charge. According literature, zeolite activation only has been performed by employing alkaline com- pounds with calcium hydroxide in particular. Nevertheless, litera- ture lacks an extensive study of the effect of Si/Al concentrations (alkaline activator (sodium hydroxide, HN)) on the geopolymeriza- tion of natural zeolitic ore. Alcántara et al. [12] carried out an investigation of cementitious material which was fabricated from natural zeolite (clinoptilolite) hydratation with calcium hydroxide (CH). The cementive materials were then characterized and compared to other activated wastes in alkaline form. The activation of clinoptilolite by alkalis produced materials with high compressive strength (38.7 MPa). The physical properties of the synthesized material depended on the prepara- tion method of the sample, curing conditions, the initial size of the particle and the concentration of the alkali. Geopolymer synthesis depends on the use of reactive raw mate- rials rich in aluminum-silicates that present a degree of thermody- namic instability which is evidenced by an amorphous structure in the material [15]. The objective of this work was to evaluate the conditions of syn- thesis for geopolymerization (sodium hydroxide concentration, so- dium silicate, time, and curing temperature) from natural zeolitic material in order to obtain cementitious materials with attractive mechanical properties similar to concrete based Portland cement. 2. Experimental 2.1. Materials Zeolite samples came from a natural deposit in Chihuahua, Mexico. The sample was homogenized, dried at 60 /C176C for 24 h, and pulverized until an average particle size 80% less than 100 lm (dp 80) was obtained. A representative sample was taken to chemical characterization by means of X-ray Spectroscopy (X- ray) atomic absorption spectroscopy (AA), gravimetric concentra- tion, and Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP). In this case, due to the fact that zeolite has a low ionic exchange index, its application was limited to use as an aggregate for concrete in the elaboration of blocks for construction. For this reason concrete made of Port- land cement, following ASTM procedures [16–18] , was used as a reference for compressive strength. Liquid sodium silicate with a 3.22 module (SiO 2/Na2O) and analytical grade sodium hydroxide was used for alkaline activation. Distilled water with a speciﬁc con- ductivity of 10 /C0 6 X/C0 1 cm/C0 1 was used. 2.2. Synthesis, testing and characterization With the objective of determining the inﬂuence of the composi- tion of the synthesized material and its effect on compressive strength, sodium silicate/sodium hydroxide in 0.4, 1.5, 5, 10 and 15 ratios were employed by using 7 M sodium hydroxide solution. Tests were carried out by using an activator/ligand ratio of 0.6. Th

Al2O3, and Fe 2O3 content in the zeolite was approximately 71.3%, which is typical of some other puzzolans such as ﬂying ash and blast furnace tailings [12]. The diffractogram of the zeolite (see Fig. 1 ) shows mainly the presence of clinoptilolite crystalline phases and, in a lesser quantity, heulandite; both which are zeo- lite-type ores. The presence of low-intensity peaks associated to al- bite (feldspar) and calcite (CaCO 3) can be seen. The halo which presents itself between 20 and 30 (2 h) indicates the presence of a fraction of amorphous material. It is worth taking in consideration that, in view of the 3% of ar- senic in zeolite, the As-dissolution was determined in the leach sulphate solution for two months, during the tests of chemical sta- bility of geopolymers (results not presented). The results show that the maximum As solubility was around 0.051 mg/L which suggests no problems of arsenic toxicity would be present for the use of this zeolite. 3.2. Compressive strength testing Fig. 2 shows the results for compressive strength of pure ce- ment, un-activated zeolite and synthesized geopolymers as a func- tion of time and curing temperature for each activator ratio (sodium silicate/sodium hydroxide). Generally, it can be observed that the synthesized geopolymers show an important increase in compressive strength the ﬁrst week of curing. They exhibit the lowest strength in tests carried out at ambient temperature. The curing time of the tests at ambient tem- perature favors strength in the geopolymer exactly as is mani- fested in the values for tests carried out for 90 days. This is in agreement with what has been reported by other authors [19]. The material synthesized at a curing temperature of 40 /C176C exhib- ited the highest compressive strength, even higher than those pre- sented by Portland cement. At temperatures higher than 60 and 80 /C176C, compressive strength is favored by short curing times, which are established in long periods (90 days). The dependence shown between strength and compressive and temperature indicates the raw material needs thermal activation in order to obtain a polymer with good compressive strength properties; at least 90 days curing time. This same effect has been reported for geopolymers based on ﬂy ash [3]. When the results which were obtained for each activator ratio with pure cement (neat cement) are compared, it can be seen that, at ambient temperature (25 /C176C), an increase in the activator ratio to 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 TEMPERATURE 80°C TEMPERATURE 60°C TEMPERATURE 40°C TEMPERATURE 25°C Curing Periods(Days) CEMENT R0.4 R1.5 R5 R10 R15 171 4 2 8 9 0 1 7 14 28 90 1 7 14 28 90 1 7 14 28 90 1 7 14 28 90 Compressive Strength (MPa) Cement Unactivated zeolite Fig. 2. 1.5 favors compressive strength of geopolymer, resulting in higher compressive values than neat cement at 90 days of curing time. Subsequent increases in the activator ratio (R5, R10 and R15) de- cline geopolymer strength. It is important to mention that, for the series of samples obtained at 40 /C176C, beginning at an activator ratio of 1.5–10, the geopolymers showed a compressive strength comparable to that shown for Portland cement, and even 11% high- er at an activation relation of 10. For the low activator ratio (0.4), the highest strengths were obtained with curing at high tempera- tures (between 60 and 80 /C176C). An increase in the activator ratio (in- crease of SS) favors mechanical strength due to the fact that the geopolymerization reaction occurs (see Eqs. (1) and (2) ) and con- tributes to the formation of silica gel; thus increasing mechanical properties [15]. This could be corroborated when the results of geopolymer and that of un-activated zeolite (in absence of SS) are compared. Un-activated zeolite treated with hydroxide results show low compressive strength indicating that SS addition and its reaction with zeolite results in a material with high compressive strength. nðSi2O5; Al2O2Þþ 2nSiO2 þ 4nH2O þ NaOH=KOH ! Naþ; Kþ þ nðOHÞ3 /C0 Si /C0 O ðOHÞ3 /C0 Al /C0 O /C0 Si /C0ð OHÞ3ðOHÞ2 ð1Þ nðOHÞ3 /C0 Si /C0 O /C0 Al /C0 ðOHÞ2 /C0 O /C0 Si /C0ð OHÞ3 þ NaOH=KOH !ð Naþ; KþÞ/C0ð /C0 Si /C0 O /C0 Al /C0 /C0 O /C0 Si /C0 O/C0Þ þ 4nH2O ð2Þ Compressive strength is affected by a high activator ratio and long curing times [15]. The drop in strength to compressive with an in- crease in SS has been reported for some systems and is due to inhi- bition from the geopolymerization reaction through Al–Si phase precipitation which prevents contact between the reacting material and the activating solution and decreases activator concentration [20]. 3.3. FTIR analysis Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra for the samples with the best results for compressive strength at different curing times and tempera- tures. It can be seen from the spectra that, as curing time elapses, a decrease in intensity at approximately 3700 cm /C0 1 occurs. At 3700/C0 1 corresponds to –OH (H bound) vibrations as well as the re- gion is between 2910 and 2840 cm /C0 1 and 1640 cm /C0 1 which corre- spond to H–O–H vibrations. The bands are linked to the presence of weak H2O bonds that are absorbed into the surface or are caught in the cavities of the geopolymeric structure[21]. This indicates that there is loss of humidity in the system in respect to inactivated zeo- lite. In the same way, a decrease in vibrations at approximately 1450, 870, and 740 cm/C0 1 is observed and corresponds to vibrations caused by the presence of Na 2CO3. This specie would be introduced in the activation solution. During the curing time, OH /C0 dissolves zeolite matrix, Na+ cations participates in the geopolymer lattice formation thus compensating the Al charges in the tetrahedral coordination. It can be observed from the results in Fig. 3 that according to curing time elapsed; the deﬁnition of the bands located at shorter wavelengths increases and is related to geopolymer formation. An increase occurs in the bands at 550 cm /C0 1 and approximately corre- sponds to the Al–O–Si symmetric tension [21,22]. Intensity of bands at 950 and 1050 cm /C0 1 are related to vibrations of the asym- metric tension of Si–O–Si and Al–O–Si groups. This is in agreement with the results obtained for the compressive st

time of 1 day at 80 /C176C of two reacted zeolite areas. As seen, the presence of Na and an increase in the Si/Al ( /C25 8) occurs with the incorporation of the activators. Furthermore, a matrix of very dense products and very little porosity can be observed. Figs. 8 and 9 show SEM micrographs and EDS results for the same geopolymer (7 M HN solution and activator ratio of 10) cured for 28 and 90 days at 40 /C176C with a dense matrix of reaction prod- ucts with very little porosity and a modiﬁcation in the Si/Al ratio which increases until 11.5 for the sample cured at 90 days; never- theless, variable compositions are observed and which indicate the presence of silica gel (with very high Si/Al ratios) and geopolymeric products with, at 28 days, a ratio of 3 was found. 4. Conclusions The results show that natural zeolite reacts with the addition of activator solution (sodium silicate and sodium hydroxide) to give rise to geopolymer formation. This is in agreement with XRD, FTIR, and SEM–EDS characterization. The analyses of the results indicate that the compressive strength of the material which was synthesized from natural zeo- lite increases to similar values of that of Portland cement. The material synthesized at a curing temperature of 40 /C176C exhibited the highest compressive strength. The dependence shown between compressive strength and temperature indicates that the raw material needs thermal activa- tion in order to obtain a geopolymer with good compressive strength properties. Furthermore, at high temperatures (60 and 80 /C176C) with short curing times also favor compressive strength. Low temperatures (25 and 40 /C176C) require long curing times in order to generate a signiﬁcant increase in strength. Acknowledgements C. in curing time in the same way that, in Fig. 4 , the phase that de- creases most is the clinoptilolite phase. 3.5. Scanning electron microscopy and energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM–EDS) characterization The following ﬁgures show the micrographs obtained of the zeolite without activation by means of SEM (see Fig. 6 ) and the geopolymer with the highest compressive strength of all systems which were studied at 1, 28, and 90 days. The micrographs were taken with the objective of analyzing their microstructural evolu- tion. The zeolite without activation had an average particle size (dp80) less than 100 lm. The Si/Al atomic ratio for the raw material was approximately 5. Fig. 7 shows the SEM micrograph and the EDS of the geopoly- mer prepared with a 7 M HN solution, activator ratio of 15, curing Fig. 7. SEM–EDS characterization of activated zeolite (geopolymer) with 7 M HN at a curing time of 24 h. The sample is the geopolymer with better compressive st rength values: an activator ratio of 15 and curing temperature of 80 /C176C. (a) SEM microphotography of the geopolymer; (b) chemical characterization by EDS of the reaction product marked as X on the photography; (c) chemical characterization by EDS of the dark area marked as Y on the photography. Fig. 8. SEM–EDS characterization of activated zeolite (geopolymer) with 7 M HN at a curing time of 28 days. The sample is the geopolymer with better compressiv e strength values: an activator ratio of 10 and curing temperature of 40 /C176C.
